fieldRegistry.label: Primary Reason For Home Health / Medical Necessity

fieldGroups.label: Medical Necessity And Homebound
fieldGroups.description: Narratives and evidence for home health need and homebound status.

fieldGroups.label: Diagnosis And Coding
fieldGroups.description: Diagnosis evidence, coding-sensitive rows, and escalation flags.

fieldGroups.label: Medications, Allergies, And Special Treatments
fieldGroups.description: Medication safety, allergies, injectables, and O0110 special treatments.

fieldGroups.label: Functional And Therapy Status
fieldGroups.description: GG/M functional scoring, therapy need, discipline frequency, and goals.

fieldGroups.label: Symptom And Body System Review
fieldGroups.description: Body-system review sections that QA checks for consistency and completeness.

fieldGroups.label: Risk And Safety Assessments
fieldGroups.description: Fall, wound, hospitalization, and safety-related risk tools.

fieldGroups.label: Care Plan And Coordination
fieldGroups.description: Plan-of-care orders, patient summary, skilled interventions, and care plan content.

fieldGroups.label: Supplementals And History
fieldGroups.description: Past medical history, caregiver support, living situation, immunizations, and supplementals.

fieldGroups.label: Low Priority Admin Reference
fieldGroups.description: Identity, agency, physician, and demographic reference values that should not dominate QA.

rotator cuff repair, subacromial decompression and arthroscopic biceps tenodesis on 4/10/26 at
Left Shoulder Rotator Cuff Tear with impingement, acromioclavicular arthritis, biceps tendinitis.
He has done 3 months of physical
therapy which only worsened his symptoms.
At this point I have recommended surgery.
decompression, distal clavicle excision, and arthroscopic biceps tenodesis.
He is active and runs daily and wants to return to golf and bowling.
Vietnam war veteran and was exposed to agent orange there.
Exam findings notable for the following:
Left shoulder exam: 90 degrees of active flexion. 4 out of 5 strength with resisted supraspinatus,
5 out of 5 with external rotation, 5 out of 5 with internal rotation.
Tenderness at the acromioclavicular joint and biceps
1 Review of the result(s) of each unique test (MRI Interpretation Shoulder (MRI Shoulder
without contrast (imaging and outside report))) and 1 Independent interpretation of a test
performed by another physician/other qualified health care professional (not separately
reported) (MRI Interpretation Shoulder (MRI Shoulder without contrast (imaging and outside

MRI of the left shoulder was reviewed, demonstrating the following findings: SDMI
Moderate supraspinatus tendinosis with low-to-moderate grade bursal-sided critical zone tear measuring 5 mm in AP
Moderate infraspinatus tendinosis with bursal-sided tendon fraying.
RW: Full-thickness tear at the insertion of the supraspinatus, impingement, biceps tendinitis.
located on the left shoulder joint.
Recommend frequency of 2-3 times per week for 6-8 weeks.
- Therapeutic Exercises: All exercises pm per therapist, Home program exercises, Strengthening exercises, and
- Manual Therapy: All exercises prn per therapist.
- Modalities: All modalities prn per therapist.
Rotator Cuff Repair Medium to Large Tears
Gradually Increase Passive Range of Motion
Flexion with elbow bent to 90 degrees
ER in Scapular Plane at 45 degrees abduction to 20-25 degrees
IR in Scapular Plane at 45 degrees abduction to 30-35 degrees
ER/IR in Scapular Plane at 45 degrees abduction
*Therapist Provides Assistance by Supporting Arm (esp. with arm lowering)
ER/IR with Arm in Scapular Plane
Initiate rhythmic stabilization ER/IR at 45 degrees abduction
Use Ice at least 6-7 times daily
Continue Sleeping in Brace until Physician Instructs
No Excessive Stretching or Sudden Movements
No Supporting of Body Weight by Hands
Continue Use of Sling or Brace (physician or therapist will determine when to discontinue)
Passive Range of Motion to Tolerance
ER at 90 degrees abduction to at least 45 degrees
IR at 90 degrees abduction to at least 45 degrees
Flexion/Extension at 100 degrees Flexion and 125 degrees flexion
Continue Use of Cryotherapy as needed
Initiate ER/IR strengthening using exercise tubing at 0 degrees of abduction (use towel roll)
Initiate Manual Resistance ER Supine in Scapular Plane (light resistance)
Initiate Prone Rowing to Neutral arm Position
Continue use of ice as needed
May use pool for light AROM exercises
May use heat prior to exercises
Especially for movements that are not full
No excessive behind the back movements
No Supporting of Body Weight by Hands & Arms
If physician permits, may initiate Light functional activities
Therapist may initiate isotonic resistance (1 lb wt.) during flexion and abduction*
Progress Golf Program to playing golf (if appropriate)
Phase V - Return to Activity Phase (Weeks 23 . 36)

Gradual Return to Recreational Sport Activities
Continue Fundamental Shoulder Exercise Program (at least 4 times weekly)
Continue Stretching, if motion is tight

Prior to providing care, providers should use the Community Care Medical Policies as a reference when determining if a Veteran meets
When additional services are requested, Community Care Medical Policies will be used to determine approval by a clinical reviewer.

the medical care for this authorization can be found below.

using the Request for Services procedures.

This authorization covers services associated with the specialty(s) identified for this episode of care, including all medical

clinically indicated (including H+P, labs, EKG, CXR, and echo when not done previously

VA Clinical Determinations and Indications (CDIs) describe standard VA health care benefits for services and procedures
that community providers may recommend as necessary for a Veteran.
Prior to providing care, providers should use the
CDIs as a reference when determining if a Veteran meets VA clinical criteria.
CDIs will be used to determine approval by a clinical reviewer.
CDIs and supporting information can be found at: https://
adaptive equipment, or other items be provided for the treatment or rehabilitation of the Veteran's medical condition.
such instances, you must complete a Request for Services (RFS) as outlined above.
The RFS must (1) indicate that the condition for which the item is being prescribed is within the scope of the authorized
(including manufacturer and model, needed custom measurements, size), and (3) provide a brief but thorough plain-
language explanation of how the item or service will serve the treatment or rehabilitation needs of the Veteran.
the RFS form must be filled out completely.
VA staff will determine whether the prescribed item or service is one that VA is authorized to purchase.

In making this determination, VA staff will consider your clinical judgment and expertise and work
with you to resolve outstanding issues quickly.
In the event of an urgent or emergent need, you may provide medical devices, necessary items or services prescribed
to Veterans receiving care in the community for urgent or emergent conditions at the time of healthcare service delivery
Urgent or emergent DME or Medical Devices may include, but are not limited to splints, crutches,
canes, slings, soft collars, walkers, and manual wheelchairs.
DME dispensed directly to the Veteran based on an urgent
or emergent need will be paid by the CCN Third Party Administrator.
Claims for these items should be submitted using the
process outlined in the Submitting Claims section of this form.

His primary diagnosis is the post-surgical condition related to the left shoulder surgery (ICD-10-CM M75.122), and he has multiple comorbidities, including type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and a history of vitamin D deficiency, which further complicate his recovery process.
